item.title: This Android compression tool for emulators frees up space for more ROMs
item.itunes:title: This Android compression tool for emulators frees up space for more ROMs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • CHDroid converts bulky ISO disc images into smaller CHD files directly on Android, saving valuable storage space.
  • Several emulators natively support CHD, allowing you to play compressed discs without decompression.
  • CHDroid also converts CHD back to ISO; the UI is bright and easy if you know Android file paths.

If you're into higher-end retro console emulation, whether it's the GameCube, the Sega Saturn, the PSP, or the PlayStation 2, or you're the type of person who likes to have nearly full ROM libraries on your Android device, CHDroid might be the perfect app for you (via Android Authority).

Disc ISOs can add up quickly, especially for games on multiple discs, which can be an issue if you have an Android phone with limited internal storage or if you prefer to keep a lot of free space on your device. This is where compression tool CHDroid comes in.

The app, which recently came out of beta with CHDroid v1.1.0, converts ISO CD images, which can sometimes be as large as 700MB or more, depending on the retro console and the game, into much smaller CHD files, allowing you to store more on your device.

CHD files are compatible with a variety of emulators

The compression app lets you fit more ISO files on your device by converting them into CHDs

Credit: PPSSPP

CHD files first appeared as a way to compress hard drive rips from arcade titles, but in this case, the file format is being used to compress ISO files so they take up less space. Several emulators support CHD files natively, including DuckStation (PS1), Flycast (Dreamcast), PPSSPP (PSP), and more, so you don't even need to unpack them before loading them into an emulator. Before CHDroid, this conversion process had to be done on a PC. However, now everything can be done directly on an Android device, which is very useful if your emulator setup is mobile-focused.

Subscribe for Retro Emulation Tips and Tool Updates

Want to fit more classic games on your phone? Subscribing to our newsletter delivers focused coverage of emulation tools, CHD conversion workflows, emulator compatibility notes, and practical Android storage tips to optimize your library.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The app even lets you convert your CHD disc image files back to ISOs if you need to. I briefly checked out CHDroid, and it's pretty easy to use, as long as you're familiar with Android's file system structure. The user interface is also bright and intuitive, which is always a plus as far as I'm concerned. You can learn more about CHDroid on the Android app's GitHub page.

You can now legally rip your Wii, GameCube, and Xbox games using a Blu-ray drive

This is great news for video game preservation.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: Your NAS is wasted if it is just a dumb storage box — you can do a lot more with it
item.itunes:title: Your NAS is wasted if it is just a dumb storage box — you can do a lot more with it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Having a NAS is no longer a niche limited to enthusiasts and those crazy about privacy. More and more users are adopting NAS devices every year, with some reports forecasting a 12% CAGR growth for the $6 billion consumer NAS market over the next four years. Data privacy and cost remain the primary drivers, as people flock to brands like Synology, QNAP, and WD for standalone NAS systems. That said, most NAS users still use it exclusively for file storage. This might seem like the primary purpose of a NAS, but it can do so much more. Once you have the right hardware, you can take your self-hosting game to a whole new level. You can also turn your NAS into a home server if you like to experiment with different operating systems and containerized applications. Your NAS enables device-agnostic data availability and redundancy, but it doesn't mean it can't encompass a host of other workloads.

NAS makers are slowly admitting people want home servers, not just storage boxes

The NAS as a concept is evolving

2
By  Ty Sherback

Your NAS is more than just an always-on data repository

It can do much more if you allow it

At its core, a NAS allows all you to access your data over your network, across all your devices. Whether you're on your PC, laptop, phone, tablet, TV, or something else, your files are at your fingertips. This not only gives you flexibility, but also supercharges internal file transfers. With 2.5GbE, 5GbE, or 10GbE ports on your NAS, your wired devices can shuttle data to and from a centralized storage server without being limited to your internet speeds. Plus, with link aggregation, you can combine the capabilities of multiple Ethernet ports on your NAS to further reduce congestion when multiple devices are vying for your NAS's attention. Your automated backups, RAW footage, media collection, work files, and miscellaneous data are protected via redundancy, thanks to RAID.

That said, your NAS isn't limited to data storage, access, and redundancy. It can become a central part of your setup, powering your productivity stack, cloud subscription substitutes, media streaming, networking stack, and even virtual machines. An always-on home server brings a lot of possibilities to the table, allowing you to expand the scope of your NAS way beyond what you bought it for. And it often doesn't need any additional investment, except when you're treading into home lab territory.

TerraMaster D4-320
$171 $190 Save $19
$171 at Amazon
My NAS does more than store files, and most people are barely using theirs

It's not just a storage box

By  Ayush Pande

Self-hosting opens up a whole new world

Open-source services can do way more than you think

Perhaps the most immediate advantage of owning a NAS is that you own your photos and videos, too, no longer relying on cloud subscription apps like Google Photos. By hosting dedicated media library services like Immich or a comprehensive cloud ecosystem like Nextcloud, you can finally remove your dependence on mega corporations to store your data. You can eliminate monthly subscription costs and ensure your media never leaves your network. Your NAS carries an initial investment, but it will pay for itself in time, especially when you include other self-hosted applications for note-taking, home automation, password management, document management, media streaming, and much more. Apps like Home Assistant, Paperless-ngx, Jellyfin, Vaultwarden, Joplin, have become hugely popular in the self-hosting community.

It doesn't have to end with productivity and entertainment, either. You can extend your NAS coverage to locally hosted ad-blocking and custom DNS services, such as Pi-hole and Technitium, respectively. Every device on your network can enjoy the benefits of a custom DNS and ad-free browsing. You can also host an NVR service like Synology Surveillance Station or open-source alternatives like Frigate. Home surveillance and media streaming are data-heavy jobs that make the best use of your NAS's storage capacity.

Immich
Key highlights
Self-hosted
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes

Immich is a self-hosted photo and video backup solution.

See at Immich
Home Assistant
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes
See at Home Assistant
I stopped treating my NAS like a file box, and it became the most useful PC in my house

Stop treating your NAS like an overpriced external drive.

11
By  Megan Ellis

Home labbers can get down and dirty

Your NAS can be whatever you need it to be

Self-hosting gives you control over your data and saves you money, but your NAS can even go a step further by turning into a home lab. If your NAS has enough RAM and a relatively modern CPU and GPU, you can host full-fledged virtual machines. You aren't limited to lightweight Docker containers, so your OS experiments aren't tied to the limitations of your host machine's OS. It might not be relevant to most users, but once your home server's complexity increases, you'll inevitably require virtualization. Separating your NAS and home server is recommended by many, but it's not impossible to host everything on your NAS, either. You may not need a home lab right now, but having a NAS makes you prepared for when you will.

Deals

Save on NAS, drives, and networking gear — deals inside

Find discounts and offers on NAS systems, HDDs/SSDs, 10GbE adapters, switches, and backup drives to upgrade your home server and self-hosting setup. Shop deals on cables, power supplies, and accessories to improve speed, redundancy, and long-term savings.
Deals Explore Storage & Networking Deals

Many NAS devices aren't powerful enough to double up as home labs, but you can build a powerful NAS yourself. Besides, manufacturers have started to realize people want to run everything on a single machine, so more capable NAS devices are now available. Of course, there's the upfront cost to consider, and most people are probably fine with more affordable but less powerful models. That said, power users who don't want to set up and maintain a separate home server will still find a unified "NAS + home lab" attractive.

I stopped using my NAS as a NAS, and it became the most useful machine in my house

My NAS became far more useful once I stopped treating it as storage and started using it as a service hub.

5
By  Jeff Butts

Don't artificially limit your NAS to file storage

You might be fine using your expensive NAS for file storage alone, but making the most of it has more than one advantage. You not only make your setup more efficient, secure, and capable, but also ensure your NAS pays for itself a lot faster. You can self-host tons of useful services and even use your NAS as a home server for more involved tinkering. Just ensure that your NAS has the necessary hardware to support your workloads.

item.title: I let Claude Code Routines babysit my home lab while I slept, and it caught what my monitoring missed
item.itunes:title: I let Claude Code Routines babysit my home lab while I slept, and it caught what my monitoring missed
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Home lab monitoring can make you feel safer than you actually are. I’ve got dashboards, alerts, logs, and enough containers doing tiny jobs that my network sometimes feels better staffed than a small office. The problem is that most monitoring tools only tell me when something crosses a line I already knew to draw. They’re great at catching outages, but they’re not always great at noticing quiet weirdness.

A routine can notice the home lab equivalent of a loose screw before the rack starts rattling.

That’s why Claude Code Routines caught my attention. The idea isn’t just asking Claude Code to fix something while I’m staring at a terminal. It’s giving it a repeatable job, letting it inspect a repository or config set, and having it report back when something looks off. For a home lab, that changes the role of AI from occasional helper to overnight reviewer, and that’s much more interesting than another dashboard widget.

Claude Code works best when you stop asking it to code

Claude Code became far more useful once I stopped treating it like a code generator and started using it to understand projects and terminal chaos.

11
By  Jeff Butts

Routine checks can find problems that monitoring ignores

The useful alerts are not always the loudest ones

Most of my monitoring is built around obvious failure. If a service goes down, Uptime Kuma can yell at me. If DNS stops resolving, Pi-hole will make the problem very clear. If a container crashes repeatedly, Docker logs will eventually turn into a crime scene. That kind of monitoring matters, but it’s still mostly binary.

The stranger problems are harder to catch. A compose file can keep working even though it has a stale image tag, a forgotten volume path, or a port mapping that made sense three months ago. A backup job can complete successfully while quietly excluding the folder I actually care about. A firewall rule can still technically work, but it becomes less readable every time I add another exception. None of those problems necessarily triggers an alert.

Claude Code Routines are useful because they can detect drift rather than just failure. I don’t need them to replace my monitoring stack, and I wouldn’t trust them as the only thing watching the lab. What I want is a second pass over the boring parts I stop seeing after the tenth revision. In that role, a routine can notice the home lab equivalent of a loose screw before the rack starts rattling.

Scheduled AI review works best as a maintenance layer

It gives messy systems a second set of eyes

The best use for Claude Code Routines isn’t handing over the keys to the whole server. It’s giving the routine a narrow, boring, repeatable job. Check Docker Compose files for inconsistent restart policies. Look for services that don’t have notes in the README. Compare expected backup targets against what’s actually mounted. These are not glamorous jobs, which is exactly why they’re easy to neglect.

That’s where the scheduled part matters. I don’t need another task that depends on me remembering to run it after midnight. I already have enough little maintenance chores hiding in corners. A routine can run when I’m asleep, summarize what it found, and leave me with a short list instead of a mystery novel. It’s not magic, but it does make maintenance feel more like a habit.

The real value is the difference between reactive and reflective monitoring. Reactive tools tell me something broke. A routine can help me ask whether the system is getting harder to maintain before it breaks. That’s a subtle shift, but it’s a useful one. In a home lab, complexity usually wins by inches, not by dramatic explosions.

Automated code agents still need tight boundaries

A helpful routine can become risky without guardrails

There’s an obvious downside here, and it’s not small. Letting an AI agent poke around your home lab configuration is not the same as running a passive uptime check. Even if the routine is only reading files, it may see service names, internal addresses, secrets that should’ve been stored elsewhere, and notes about how the network is stitched together. If it can make changes, the stakes get higher fast.

Don’t give Claude Code Routines direct access to live credentials, production secrets, or anything that can change a running service until you’ve tested the workflow carefully. Start with read-only reviews of sanitized config files, then treat any suggested fixes as proposals to be approved manually. The point is to catch drift and weak spots before they become outages, not to hand your home lab a robot with root access and a flashlight.

That means the first rule is scope. A routine should only see the repositories and files it actually needs for the job. It shouldn’t have access to production secrets, live credentials, or anything that would turn a bad prompt into a bad weekend. I’d rather copy sanitized configs into a review repo than point an automated agent at the real nerve center. Convenience is great until it starts wearing admin credentials as a hat.

There’s also the trust problem. Claude Code can be impressively good at explaining why something looks wrong, but it can still misunderstand intent. A weird port mapping might be deliberate. A backup exclusion might be there because that folder is rebuilt from scratch every night. A routine can flag those choices, but it shouldn’t silently “fix” them unless the task is extremely narrow and the rollback path is obvious.

The right setup makes this safer and more useful

Treat routines like junior admins with locked doors

The way to make Claude Code Routines useful is to treat them like a cautious reviewer, not an autonomous sysadmin. I’d want one routine for compose hygiene, another for backup sanity checks, and another for documentation drift. Each routine should have its own prompt, scope, and expected output. That keeps the results readable and makes failures easier to understand.

I’d also avoid permitting routines to make live changes at first. A nightly report is useful enough on its own, especially if it tells me which file needs attention and why. Once a routine has proven reliable, I might let it open a proposed change in a repository, but I’d still review it before anything touches a running service. That extra step keeps the workflow useful without turning my home lab into an unattended experiment.

This is also where routines can beat normal monitoring without pretending to replace it. Monitoring watches the service from the outside. A routine can inspect the service’s intent from the inside. When both are working together, I get a better picture than either one provides alone. One tells me whether the light is on, and the other tells me whether the wiring behind it is starting to look suspicious.

This is not autopilot, but it is real progress

Claude Code Routines don’t make a home lab self-healing, and I don’t want them to. A self-hosted setup is too personal, too weird, and too full of tiny exceptions for that kind of blind confidence. What they can do is make maintenance less memory-dependent. That matters because most home lab failures don’t start as failures.

They start as little messes we promise to clean up later. A stale compose file here, a missing note there, a service that still works but no longer matches the way the rest of the stack is organized. Claude Code Routines give those little messes a place to surface before they become a Saturday repair project. That’s not a replacement for monitoring, but it’s the layer I didn’t realize my home lab needed.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
Group pricing
$100/month per person for the Max plan

Claude Code Routines can make excellent assistants in monitoring your home lab's health.

item.description:
Home lab monitoring can make you feel safer than you actually are. I’ve got dashboards, alerts, logs, and enough containers doing tiny jobs that my network sometimes feels better staffed than a small office. The problem is that most monitoring tools only tell me when something crosses a line I already knew to draw. They’re great at catching outages, but they’re not always great at noticing quiet weirdness.

A routine can notice the home lab equivalent of a loose screw before the rack starts rattling.

That’s why Claude Code Routines caught my attention. The idea isn’t just asking Claude Code to fix something while I’m staring at a terminal. It’s giving it a repeatable job, letting it inspect a repository or config set, and having it report back when something looks off. For a home lab, that changes the role of AI from occasional helper to overnight reviewer, and that’s much more interesting than another dashboard widget.

Claude Code works best when you stop asking it to code

Claude Code became far more useful once I stopped treating it like a code generator and started using it to understand projects and terminal chaos.

11
By  Jeff Butts

Routine checks can find problems that monitoring ignores

The useful alerts are not always the loudest ones

Most of my monitoring is built around obvious failure. If a service goes down, Uptime Kuma can yell at me. If DNS stops resolving, Pi-hole will make the problem very clear. If a container crashes repeatedly, Docker logs will eventually turn into a crime scene. That kind of monitoring matters, but it’s still mostly binary.

The stranger problems are harder to catch. A compose file can keep working even though it has a stale image tag, a forgotten volume path, or a port mapping that made sense three months ago. A backup job can complete successfully while quietly excluding the folder I actually care about. A firewall rule can still technically work, but it becomes less readable every time I add another exception. None of those problems necessarily triggers an alert.

Claude Code Routines are useful because they can detect drift rather than just failure. I don’t need them to replace my monitoring stack, and I wouldn’t trust them as the only thing watching the lab. What I want is a second pass over the boring parts I stop seeing after the tenth revision. In that role, a routine can notice the home lab equivalent of a loose screw before the rack starts rattling.

Scheduled AI review works best as a maintenance layer

It gives messy systems a second set of eyes

The best use for Claude Code Routines isn’t handing over the keys to the whole server. It’s giving the routine a narrow, boring, repeatable job. Check Docker Compose files for inconsistent restart policies. Look for services that don’t have notes in the README. Compare expected backup targets against what’s actually mounted. These are not glamorous jobs, which is exactly why they’re easy to neglect.

That’s where the scheduled part matters. I don’t need another task that depends on me remembering to run it after midnight. I already have enough little maintenance chores hiding in corners. A routine can run when I’m asleep, summarize what it found, and leave me with a short list instead of a mystery novel. It’s not magic, but it does make maintenance feel more like a habit.

The real value is the difference between reactive and reflective monitoring. Reactive tools tell me something broke. A routine can help me ask whether the system is getting harder to maintain before it breaks. That’s a subtle shift, but it’s a useful one. In a home lab, complexity usually wins by inches, not by dramatic explosions.

Automated code agents still need tight boundaries

A helpful routine can become risky without guardrails

There’s an obvious downside here, and it’s not small. Letting an AI agent poke around your home lab configuration is not the same as running a passive uptime check. It shouldn’t have access to production secrets, live credentials, or anything that would turn a bad prompt into a bad weekend. I’d rather copy sanitized configs into a review repo than point an automated agent at the real nerve center. Convenience is great until it starts wearing admin credentials as a hat.

There’s also the trust problem. Claude Code can be impressively good at explaining why something looks wrong, but it can still misunderstand intent. A weird port mapping might be deliberate. A backup exclusion might be there because that folder is rebuilt from scratch every night. A routine can flag those choices, but it shouldn’t silently “fix” them unless the task is extremely narrow and the rollback path is obvious.

Treat routines like junior admins with locked doors

The way to make Claude Code Routines useful is to treat them like a cautious reviewer, not an autonomous sysadmin. I’d want one routine for compose hygiene, another for backup sanity checks, and another for documentation drift. Each routine should have its own prompt, scope, and expected output. That keeps the results readable and makes failures easier to understand.

I’d also avoid permitting routines to make live changes at first. A nightly report is useful enough on its own, especially if it tells me which file needs attention and why. Once a routine has proven reliable, I might let it open a proposed change in a repository, but I’d still review it before anything touches a running service. That extra step keeps the workflow useful without turning my home lab into an unattended experiment.

This is also where routines can beat normal monitoring without pretending to replace it. Monitoring watches the service from the outside. A routine can inspect the service’s intent from the inside. When both are working together, I get a better picture than either one provides alone. One tells me whether the light is on, and the other tells me whether the wiring behind it is starting to look suspicious.

This is not autopilot, but it is real progress

Claude Code Routines don’t make a home lab self-healing, and I don’t want them to. A self-hosted setup is too personal, too weird, and too full of tiny exceptions for that kind of blind confidence. What they can do is make maintenance less memory-dependent. That matters because most home lab failures don’t start as failures.

They start as little messes we promise to clean up later. A stale compose file here, a missing note there, a service that still works but no longer matches the way the rest of the stack is organized. Claude Code Routines give those little messes a place to surface before they become a Saturday repair project. That’s not a replacement for monitoring, but it’s the layer I didn’t realize my home lab needed.

item.title: I run every sketchy installer in Windows Sandbox, and it's saved me twice this year
item.itunes:title: I run every sketchy installer in Windows Sandbox, and it's saved me twice this year
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There's no dearth of malware hiding in plain sight or poorly designed apps. Windows has built-in tools to combat most of this, but some malware can still creep into your system. The risk increases for users like me who download and test software daily. Some of them are actively updated and maintained, while others can be experimental or very outdated. The traditional way to stay protected is to use a combination of antivirus software and to run every new app in an isolated environment.

A virtual machine is a good idea to test apps, but that's mildly inconvenient. You need to create a machine, dedicate resources to it, and also manage it. So, I use Windows Sandbox, a tool that creates an isolated environment within Windows and is a perfect testing ground for apps and software.

Windows Sandbox remains unnoticed

Partially Microsoft’s fault

Windows Sandbox is less popular than other features, mainly because it’s related to virtualization. Your Windows computer uses virtualization to enforce a few security practices, and unless you play with virtual machines, you never notice the option in your system. Another reason for Windows Sandbox’s low popularity is Microsoft’s fault. It deliberately restricts the feature to the Pro edition of Windows.

When you buy a new Windows 11 PC, there’s a high chance it comes with the Home edition rather than the Pro edition. It’s Microsoft’s idea to upsell the Pro version for a few more features, but Sandbox should be part of all Windows editions. Sandbox has protected me quite a few times from falling prey to sketchy app installs and scripts, and it saves me the effort of using a virtual machine for the same purpose.

If Sandbox became a standard practice, users wouldn’t need to learn the concept of hypervisors and virtualization. They could run the downloaded setup file or script in the Sandbox environment to check how it impacts the system before installing it on the main machines. The isolated area is a lifesaver that remains separate from your main OS files, and Windows destroys it immediately after you end the Sandbox session.

Stop downloading software from websites: Windows has a built-in package manager

Make things easier for yourself

57
By  João Carrasqueira

My Sandbox experience

Necessary for theming tools and scripts

Using Sandbox has saved my work PC multiple times this year. The first incident involved a theming tool that I downloaded for a post that covered tinkering with Windows themes. It redirected me to a not-so-popular website, and since it was urgent, I downloaded it. But I don’t trust apps unless they are from the Microsoft Store or official websites, and I decided to give it a go in Sandbox.

The first run didn’t go well and immediately destabilized the Sandbox environment. It was so bad that the Sandbox system stopped registering clicks and went blank. Usually, I try theme apps directly, but since it was a combination of several apps and theme files, Sandbox seemed like a better option. If I had done that on my main PC, I would have lost all my progress and would have had to resort to WinRE to try to fix the PC.

A few weeks later, I found myself in the same predicament, but this time with a PowerShell script. The results weren’t what the script promised, and it seemed to download a lot of unnecessary stuff in the background. If I ran it with administrator rights on my main system, the results would have been catastrophic. It would have downloaded files to multiple system directories, and cleaning them up would become a monumental challenge.

I use PowerShell scripts to disable Windows features, remove AI clutter, and curb tracking practices. Win11Debloat is my favorite suite for this on every new Windows 11 install, but after this incident, I never ran a script directly. Since most of them need administrator privileges, running them in Sandbox makes more sense.

Sandbox is convenient

No need for virtual machines

Using preventive measures for new apps and scripts should become a common practice, and Windows Sandbox can help with it. An experienced user wouldn’t mind searching forums for problems related to an app, or using a dedicated virtual machine or a spare system to validate the concerns about it.

However, a regular Windows user who uses a single computer for work and home cannot afford such extreme methods. Creating and using virtual machines sounds cumbersome. No one would prefer to launch another Windows copy that runs and loads very slowly on a virtual machine, and then test an app inside it.

Running it in Sandbox is straightforward, with no risk or complexity for end users. You can open a browser inside the Sandbox, download the install, and test the app to your heart’s content. If you notice problems during this phase, you can skip the app installation on your main system altogether.

Deals

Deals on PCs, security software, and work-setup savings

Explore discounts on hardware, security suites, RAM, and SSDs to build a safer testing setup—plus offers on virtualization-ready laptops, docking stations, and peripherals. Check Computers & Work Setup deals to lock in savings and upgrade protection affordably.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The same goes for running scripts that introduce automation, disable a system policy, or manipulate the registry. You don’t need to even worry about creating restore points because each Sandbox session is separate and won’t affect your system or personal files. Everything inside it vanishes after you close the app.

There are third-party Sandbox tools like Sandboxie-Plus that use the same concept, but I found them very confusing to use. The UI expects you to be familiar with such tools, and creating and running a new Sandbox environment is tougher than the natively available Windows Sandbox.

I fiddled with the settings multiple times before getting the first Sandbox live, and never really used the app again. Windows Sandbox’s approach is to make novice users comfortable without having to worry about resource allocation or storage settings.

Make Windows Sandbox a habit

I suggest using Windows Sandbox regularly if you own a copy of Windows 11 Pro. It’s one of the biggest perks of paying extra for the OS, since Windows is a prime target for many malicious tools and apps. Even if you follow standard security practices, an urgent app installation from a less trustworthy source can still introduce problems. It is simpler than using a virtual machine tool like Hyper-V or VirtualBox, and requires no additional configuration except a one-time setup.

Windows 11 Pro

A USB installation drive and license key for Windows 11 Pro, with additional features like Hyper-V and Windows Sandbox support.

See at Microsoft

But I don’t trust apps unless they are from the Microsoft Store or official websites, and I decided to give it a go in Sandbox.

The first run didn’t go well and immediately destabilized the Sandbox environment. It was so bad that the Sandbox system stopped registering clicks and went blank. Usually, I try theme apps directly, but since it was a combination of several apps and theme files, Sandbox seemed like a better option. If I had done that on my main PC, I would have lost all my progress and would have had to resort to WinRE to try to fix the PC.

A few weeks later, I found myself in the same predicament, but this time with a PowerShell script. The results weren’t what the script promised, and it seemed to download a lot of unnecessary stuff in the background. If I ran it with administrator rights on my main system, the results would have been catastrophic. It would have downloaded files to multiple system directories, and cleaning them up would become a monumental challenge.

I use PowerShell scripts to disable Windows features, remove AI clutter, and curb tracking practices. Win11Debloat is my favorite suite for this on every new Windows 11 install, but after this incident, I never ran a script directly. Since most of them need administrator privileges, running them in Sandbox makes more sense.

Sandbox is convenient

No need for virtual machines

Using preventive measures for new apps and scripts should become a common practice, and Windows Sandbox can help with it. An experienced user wouldn’t mind searching forums for problems related to an app, or using a dedicated virtual machine or a spare system to validate the concerns about it.

However, a regular Windows user who uses a single computer for work and home cannot afford such extreme methods. Creating and using virtual machines sounds cumbersome. No one would prefer to launch another Windows copy that runs and loads very slowly on a virtual machine, and then test an app inside it.

Running it in Sandbox is straightforward, with no risk or complexity for end users. You can open a browser inside the Sandbox, download the install, and test the app to your heart’s content. If you notice problems during this phase, you can skip the app installation on your main system altogether.

Deals

Deals on PCs, security software, and work-setup savings

Explore discounts on hardware, security suites, RAM, and SSDs to build a safer testing setup—plus offers on virtualization-ready laptops, docking stations, and peripherals. Check Computers & Work Setup deals to lock in savings and upgrade protection affordably.
Deals Explore Computers & Work Setup Deals

The same goes for running scripts that introduce automation, disable a system policy, or manipulate the registry. You don’t need to even worry about creating restore points because each Sandbox session is separate and won’t affect your system or personal files. Everything inside it vanishes after you close the app.

There are third-party Sandbox tools like Sandboxie-Plus that use the same concept, but I found them very confusing to use. The UI expects you to be familiar with such tools, and creating and running a new Sandbox environment is tougher than the natively available Windows Sandbox.

I fiddled with the settings multiple times before getting the first Sandbox live, and never really used the app again. Windows Sandbox’s approach is to make novice users comfortable without having to worry about resource allocation or storage settings.

Make Windows Sandbox a habit

I suggest using Windows Sandbox regularly if you own a copy of Windows 11 Pro. It’s one of the biggest perks of paying extra for the OS, since Windows is a prime target for many malicious tools and apps. Even if you follow standard security practices, an urgent app installation from a less trustworthy source can still introduce problems. It is simpler than using a virtual machine tool like Hyper-V or VirtualBox, and requires no additional configuration except a one-time setup.

Windows 11 Pro

A USB installation drive and license key for Windows 11 Pro, with additional features like Hyper-V and Windows Sandbox support.

See at Microsoft

item.title: AMD's X3D CPUs aged so well that gamers won't upgrade, and that's a nightmare for AM5
item.itunes:title: AMD's X3D CPUs aged so well that gamers won't upgrade, and that's a nightmare for AM5
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You might think AMD's biggest concern today is the possibility of Intel catching up with its upcoming Nova Lake CPUs. While Intel's long-awaited response to X3D CPUs could finally make the gaming CPU market more competitive again, I honestly don't think that's AMD's biggest problem right now. In fact, AMD's biggest challenge might actually be something it created itself. Its older AM4 CPUs are still too good for the vast majority of gamers.

When you have millions still sitting on AM4 builds, it's hard to convince them to spend almost a grand on the newer AM5 platform, especially with RAM prices soaring over the last few months. And at this point, I can't blame them for wanting to hold on to AM4 longer. The 5800X3D, for example, is still powerful enough to pair with a high-end GPU without serious bottlenecks. Of course, the 9800X3D is a lot faster, but at higher resolutions like 1440p and 4K, the gap just isn't big enough to feel left behind.

4 ways you can optimize your AM4 setup for gaming in 2026

These four can breathe some more life into AM4

2
By  Abhinav Raj

AM4's best CPUs made drop-in upgrades too appealing

Why splurge on AM5 when an older X3D chip still gets you most of the gains?

One of the biggest reasons AM4 has aged so well is that AMD gave the platform a genuinely meaningful final upgrade path. Instead of forcing people to rebuild their entire PCs, X3D CPUs like the Ryzen 7 5800X3D and 5700X3D let existing AM4 users squeeze much more gaming performance out of the systems they already own. That's a huge deal when you consider how much it costs to upgrade to AM5 today. You're spending more on DDR5 memory than you would for a 9800X3D. And let's not forget the cost of the motherboard either.

So, if you have a Zen 2 or even a Zen 3 non-X3D CPU, it makes more sense to upgrade to an X3D CPU and max out what AM4 has to offer before thinking about AM5. Sure, if you have a 12-core or 16-core CPU, you're basically sacrificing multi-core performance, but for gaming, that extra L3 cache matters so much more if there's one thing I've learned from sidegrading from a 5900X to a 5800X3D. If it wasn't for AM4's longevity, I probably would've moved to AM5 a lot sooner, and I believe many of you feel the same way.

3D V-Cache changed how long CPUs stay relevant

Even the 9800X3D doesn't make the 5800X3D obsolete outside of benchmarks

The 5800X3D came out over four years ago, but it still handles almost everything I throw at it. And that's mostly because of its whopping 96MB of L3 cache stacked directly on top of the CPU cores. At the time, nobody knew that extra cache would end up changing how long gaming CPUs stay relevant, especially for those who don't constantly chase the latest CPU every generation. Before the era of 3D V-Cache, you'd typically have to upgrade your CPU at least every other generation to make sure it keeps up with high-end GPUs.

Fast-forward to 2026, and the 5800X3D is still good enough for the RTX 4090. Sure, when you look at benchmarks for the 9800X3D, you might feel like the 5800X3D is outdated, but keep in mind those tests are run at 1080p to expose CPU bottlenecks. At 1440p and especially 4K, you're far more likely to be GPU-bound anyway, so you don't really need the fastest CPU on the market to keep up with it. That's exactly why the 5800X3D still doesn't feel nearly as old as a four-year-old CPU normally should for gaming.

There are still reasons to look beyond AM4

But for most gamers who want solid performance, AM4 still doesn't disappoint

I'm not saying that AM5 isn't worthwhile just because AM4's X3D CPUs are still very capable. If you're chasing very high frame rates, say with a 360Hz monitor, you will need something like the 9800X3D or 9950X3D. In those scenarios, your CPU is the limiting factor, not your GPU. In fact, if you have the RTX 5090, the 5800X3D might end up being a bottleneck at 1440p. Moreover, upgrading to AM5 isn't just about chasing faster CPUs. You're getting support for PCIe 5.0, faster DDR5 memory, USB 4, and even Wi-Fi 7 on newer boards. So, if you want your PC to feel future-proof, AM5 absolutely makes sense.

Subscribe for clear AM4 vs AM5 upgrade guidance

Make upgrade decisions easier: subscribe to the newsletter for focused analysis on AM4 vs AM5 trade-offs, X3D longevity, and practical cost breakdowns (DDR5, motherboards, GPU bottlenecks). Get clear criteria you can use when weighing whether to upgrade your PC.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

That said, the vast majority of people aren't gaming on an RTX 5090 or aiming for 200+ FPS. The reality is that many gamers are still using mid-range GPUs, where the difference between a 5800X3D and 9800X3D becomes much harder to notice. So, when AM4 already offers CPUs that don't bottleneck your GPU, there's no need to rush to AM5. Yes, DDR5 memory can help, but you need to be CPU-limited in the first place for that to matter. And PCIe 5.0 SSDs aren't going to load games any faster than older Gen 4 drives, so you're really not leaving much on the table by sticking with AM4.

AM4 aged a little too well for AMD's own good

Nobody expected AM4 to remain relevant for a decade, especially in an industry where people are used to upgrading their CPUs every two or three years. In fact, when the Ryzen 5000-series CPUs first came out, many people thought it was the end of the line for AM4. But X3D CPUs went on to give the platform a second life. Despite much faster CPUs available today, they still hold their own in the latest games. Of course, upgrading to AM5 may be inevitable at some point, but for now, AMD's older X3D CPUs are still so good that many people don't feel the urge to splurge on a new board and RAM kit.

AM4 might still be supported when AM6 launches, but that doesn’t mean you should buy into the platform now

AMD's support of AM4 has been impressive, but something has to give

2
By  Ty Sherback
item.description:

You're getting support for PCIe 5.0, faster DDR5 memory, USB 4, and even Wi-Fi 7 on newer boards. So, if you want your PC to feel future-proof, AM5 absolutely makes sense.

Subscribe for clear AM4 vs AM5 upgrade guidance

Make upgrade decisions easier: subscribe to the newsletter for focused analysis on AM4 vs AM5 trade-offs, X3D longevity, and practical cost breakdowns (DDR5, motherboards, GPU bottlenecks). Get clear criteria you can use when weighing whether to upgrade your PC.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

That said, the vast majority of people aren't gaming on an RTX 5090 or aiming for 200+ FPS. The reality is that many gamers are still using mid-range GPUs, where the difference between a 5800X3D and 9800X3D becomes much harder to notice. So, when AM4 already offers CPUs that don't bottleneck your GPU, there's no need to rush to AM5. Yes, DDR5 memory can help, but you need to be CPU-limited in the first place for that to matter. And PCIe 5.0 SSDs aren't going to load games any faster than older Gen 4 drives, so you're really not leaving much on the table by sticking with AM4.

AM4 aged a little too well for AMD's own good

Nobody expected AM4 to remain relevant for a decade, especially in an industry where people are used to upgrading their CPUs every two or three years. In fact, when the Ryzen 5000-series CPUs first came out, many people thought it was the end of the line for AM4. But X3D CPUs went on to give the platform a second life. item.title: The Nvidia Shield TV is old, but every replacement still gets one thing wrong
item.itunes:title: The Nvidia Shield TV is old, but every replacement still gets one thing wrong
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Nvidia Shield TV is one of those rare examples of hardware that was way overpowered for its time and price.

There is no doubt it is old, but it was so far ahead of its time that even today, there are things no other replacement can do. Of course, the Nvidia Shield itself can easily keep up with the best of streamers. Recently, I picked up an Apple TV 4K, and as great as it is, it made me think there are still a few things no other streamer gets right. You see, most streaming devices today focus on delivering content, but the Nvidia Shield was built to improve the experience around that content.

My media streaming setup shouldn't be unusual for anyone in the self-hosting world. I use it for Netflix and YouTube, but my setup is also tied into a NAS full of movies and TV shows. Plex handles the library, and I still watch a lot of older content that isn't available on streaming or that doesn't have 4K upscaling.

Similarly, having recently invested in a decent audio setup, I do care about audio quality. As good as my Apple TV is, it's clear to me that the Nvidia Shield, even in 2026, remains the superior product. Honestly, I am surprised, but here we are.

Most of what I watch isn't and won't be in native 4K

Nvidia's AI upscaling continues to hold up

Let's talk about the content problem first. It's easy to assume that 4K is everywhere now, but unless all you watch is the absolute latest programming, that's absolutely not the case. Even right up till the mid-2015-2016 years, most of the content was shot at 1080p. If you go further back to the golden era of television and movies and even YouTube videos, documentaries, or any other online content that you might have saved, forget about 4K. Quality can vary wildly depending on bit rate and compression, and finding even 1080p video can be a bit of a task.

Normally, when this lower-resolution content is stretched across a large television screen, it absolutely shows its age. The textures look softer, there are no sharp edges, and everything is just a little low-resolution, which makes sense given the low-resolution. While modern TVs do their own processing using built-in upscaling chips, you might not have the absolute latest TV on the market. In fact, I'd say that TV upscaling has only gotten pretty good over the last two to three years.

Meanwhile, the Nvidia Shield Pro handles upscaling differently, thanks to its AI-enhanced features. Before AI was everywhere, Nvidia launched AI-based upscaling with the 2nd-generation Nvidia Shield Pro, training it to analyze lower-resolution video and rebuild details before sending the final image to the TV. Think of it a bit like DLSS but for your media.

This feature works astoundingly well and has absolutely no replacement for any other streamer. You also don't need to actively twiddle around with settings. Just leave it enabled and continue watching; the upscaling algorithms will make faces look cleaner, and small details will be a little sharper. The same goes for textures in clothing and hair.

That, of course, does not mean it can perform miracles if the source file is extremely low-resolution or low-bitrate. The Shield TV cannot create information that does not exist. Upscaling can cause artifacts or sharpening once in a while, but for the most part, it can make your old content look surprisingly crisp, if not native 4K-like. I wish my Apple TV or even other Android-based streaming boxes had an equivalent solution.

A $40 streaming stick fixed everything wrong with my smart TV

Amazon's Fire TV Stick 4K is far more capable than I expected.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Streaming boxes still struggle with one part of home theater

The Nvidia Shield plays audio the way it should

The other aspect of the Shield TV that continues to stand out even in 2026 is its broad support for high-end audio formats. I know everybody focuses on picture quality, but in my opinion, great audio is what truly sells it. Having only recently upgraded to a pretty decent set of speakers, I can attest to how much of a difference it makes to your media consumption.

However, most streaming services use compressed audio formats to keep bandwidth usage reasonable and reduce file sizes. It might be fine on a smartphone or even for just basic watching, but if you're ripping a Blu-ray disc or downloading a high-quality remux, these files include lossless formats like Dolby TrueHD and DTS:X. If you've invested in high-end speakers, you want to hear this audio.

Nvidia officially supports pass-through for formats including Dolby TrueHD, Dolby Atmos, DTS:X, and DTS streams over HDMI. Once again, astonishingly, a wide range of modern streamers do not. Moreover, even if a streaming box supports one or a few of these formats, you'll often encounter glitches when the audio is still being converted. That's not something that you want to deal with on a daily basis. However, when I use the Nvidia Shield TV every day, there is a feature I've noticed that's absolutely rock solid. If you want a streamer as the central part of your home theater system, for better or worse, there is still no replacement for the Nvidia Shield TV.

Seven years later, the Nvidia Shield TV has no direct replacement

Look, in any other scenario, suggesting that you should buy a rather ancient piece of technology in 2026 as the central pillar of your entertainment system would be bad advice. I would never suggest that, but as it turns out, the Nvidia Shield is still being updated and can still keep up pace with everything else on the market. It has a range of features that no one else offers.

It blows my mind that no other brand has managed to build a product that can truly replace the Nvidia Shield, but that's the truth of it. It can make your old content look better and handle audio without turning playback into a troubleshooting session. All of that, combined with the otherwise excellent streaming capabilities, ensures that this seven-year-old streaming box from 2019 has no replacement available even today.

Nvidia Shield TV Pro

The Nvidia Shield TV Pro costs $50 more than the regular model but it comes with some additional features to add more value to the overall package.

$200 at Amazon
I stopped trying to replace my Nvidia Shield and built something better instead

The Shield did it all, and that approach has its problems.

13
By  Dhruv Bhutani
item.description:

I wish my Apple TV or even other Android-based streaming boxes had an equivalent solution.

A $40 streaming stick fixed everything wrong with my smart TV

Amazon's Fire TV Stick 4K is far more capable than I expected.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Streaming boxes still struggle with one part of home theater

The Nvidia Shield plays audio the way it should

The other aspect of the Shield TV that continues to stand out even in 2026 is its broad support for high-end audio formats. I know everybody focuses on picture quality, but in my opinion, great audio is what truly sells it. Having only recently upgraded to a pretty decent set of speakers, I can attest to how much of a difference it makes to your media consumption.

However, most streaming services use compressed audio formats to keep bandwidth usage reasonable and reduce file sizes. It might be fine on a smartphone or even for just basic watching, but if you're ripping a Blu-ray disc or downloading a high-quality remux, these files include lossless formats like Dolby TrueHD and DTS:X. If you've invested in high-end speakers, you want to hear this audio.

Nvidia officially supports pass-through for formats including Dolby TrueHD, Dolby Atmos, DTS:X, and DTS streams over HDMI. Once again, astonishingly, a wide range of modern streamers do not. Moreover, even if a streaming box supports one or a few of these formats, you'll often encounter glitches when the audio is still being converted. That's not something that you want to deal with on a daily basis. However, when I use the Nvidia Shield TV every day, there is a feature I've noticed that's absolutely rock solid. If you want a streamer as the central part of your home theater system, for better or worse, there is still no replacement for the Nvidia Shield TV.

Seven years later, the Nvidia Shield TV has no direct replacement

Look, in any other scenario, suggesting that you should buy a rather ancient piece of technology in 2026 as the central pillar of your entertainment system would be bad advice. I would never suggest that, but as it turns out, the Nvidia Shield is still being updated and can still keep up pace with everything else on the market. It has a range of features that no one else offers.

It blows my mind that no other brand has managed to build a product that can truly replace the Nvidia Shield, but that's the truth of it. It can make your old content look better and handle audio without turning playback into a troubleshooting session. All of that, combined with the otherwise excellent streaming capabilities, ensures that this seven-year-old streaming box from 2019 has no replacement available even today.

Nvidia Shield TV Pro

The Nvidia Shield TV Pro costs $50 more than the regular model but it comes with some additional features to add more value to the overall package.

$200 at Amazon
I stopped trying to replace my Nvidia Shield and built something better instead

The Shield did it all, and that approach has its problems.

13
By  Dhruv Bhutani
item.content:encoded:
The Nvidia Shield TV is one of those rare examples of hardware that was way overpowered for its time and price.

There is no doubt it is old, but it was so far ahead of its time that even today, there are things no other replacement can do. Of course, the Nvidia Shield itself can easily keep up with the best of streamers. Recently, I picked up an Apple TV 4K, and as great as it is, it made me think there are still a few things no other streamer gets right. You see, most streaming devices today focus on delivering content, but the Nvidia Shield was built to improve the experience around that content.

My media streaming setup shouldn't be unusual for anyone in the self-hosting world. I use it for Netflix and YouTube, but my setup is also tied into a NAS full of movies and TV shows. Plex handles the library, and I still watch a lot of older content that isn't available on streaming or that doesn't have 4K upscaling.

Similarly, having recently invested in a decent audio setup, I do care about audio quality. As good as my Apple TV is, it's clear to me that the Nvidia Shield, even in 2026, remains the superior product. Honestly, I am surprised, but here we are.

Most of what I watch isn't and won't be in native 4K

Nvidia's AI upscaling continues to hold up

Let's talk about the content problem first. It's easy to assume that 4K is everywhere now, but unless all you watch is the absolute latest programming, that's absolutely not the case. Even right up till the mid-2015-2016 years, most of the content was shot at 1080p. If you go further back to the golden era of television and movies and even YouTube videos, documentaries, or any other online content that you might have saved, forget about 4K. Quality can vary wildly depending on bit rate and compression, and finding even 1080p video can be a bit of a task.

Normally, when this lower-resolution content is stretched across a large television screen, it absolutely shows its age. The textures look softer, there are no sharp edges, and everything is just a little low-resolution, which makes sense given the low-resolution. While modern TVs do their own processing using built-in upscaling chips, you might not have the absolute latest TV on the market. In fact, I'd say that TV upscaling has only gotten pretty good over the last two to three years.

Meanwhile, the Nvidia Shield Pro handles upscaling differently, thanks to its AI-enhanced features. Before AI was everywhere, Nvidia launched AI-based upscaling with the 2nd-generation Nvidia Shield Pro, training it to analyze lower-resolution video and rebuild details before sending the final image to the TV. Think of it a bit like DLSS but for your media.

This feature works astoundingly well and has absolutely no replacement for any other streamer. You also don't need to actively twiddle around with settings. Just leave it enabled and continue watching; the upscaling algorithms will make faces look cleaner, and small details will be a little sharper. The same goes for textures in clothing and hair.

That, of course, does not mean it can perform miracles if the source file is extremely low-resolution or low-bitrate. The Shield TV cannot create information that does not exist. Upscaling can cause artifacts or sharpening once in a while, but for the most part, it can make your old content look surprisingly crisp, if not native 4K-like. I wish my Apple TV or even other Android-based streaming boxes had an equivalent solution.

A $40 streaming stick fixed everything wrong with my smart TV

Amazon's Fire TV Stick 4K is far more capable than I expected.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Streaming boxes still struggle with one part of home theater

The Nvidia Shield plays audio the way it should

The other aspect of the Shield TV that continues to stand out even in 2026 is its broad support for high-end audio formats. I know everybody focuses on picture quality, but in my opinion, great audio is what truly sells it. Having only recently upgraded to a pretty decent set of speakers, I can attest to how much of a difference it makes to your media consumption.

However, most streaming services use compressed audio formats to keep bandwidth usage reasonable and reduce file sizes. It might be fine on a smartphone or even for just basic watching, but if you're ripping a Blu-ray disc or downloading a high-quality remux, these files include lossless formats like Dolby TrueHD and DTS:X. If you've invested in high-end speakers, you want to hear this audio.

Nvidia officially supports pass-through for formats including Dolby TrueHD, Dolby Atmos, DTS:X, and DTS streams over HDMI. Once again, astonishingly, a wide range of modern streamers do not. Moreover, even if a streaming box supports one or a few of these formats, you'll often encounter glitches when the audio is still being converted. That's not something that you want to deal with on a daily basis. However, when I use the Nvidia Shield TV every day, there is a feature I've noticed that's absolutely rock solid. If you want a streamer as the central part of your home theater system, for better or worse, there is still no replacement for the Nvidia Shield TV.

It blows my mind that no other brand has managed to build a product that can truly replace the Nvidia Shield, but that's the truth of it. All of that, combined with the otherwise excellent streaming capabilities, ensures that this seven-year-old streaming box from 2019 has no replacement available even today.

Nvidia Shield TV Pro

The Nvidia Shield TV Pro costs $50 more than the regular model but it comes with some additional features to add more value to the overall package.

$200 at Amazon
I stopped trying to replace my Nvidia Shield and built something better instead

The Shield did it all, and that approach has its problems.

13
By  Dhruv Bhutani

item.title: Your old laptop is too weak for Windows 11, but it's strong enough for home lab VMs and containers
item.itunes:title: Your old laptop is too weak for Windows 11, but it's strong enough for home lab VMs and containers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you’re a newcomer to the home lab ecosystem, you might believe bulky rigs and ex-enterprise systems are the only hardware that could keep up with typical virtualization tools. But in reality, you can turn practically any functional PC into a reliable server node for your home lab experiments, including dinosaur systems that can’t meet Windows 11’s minimum requirements.

In fact, turning an aged laptop into a home server is one of the best ways you can breathe new life into your computing companion. Sure, there are a couple of caveats to using a laptop as a dedicated workstation (some more dire than others), but as long as you exercise due caution, it’s more than enough for home lab experiments.

Don't just revive your old laptop, turn it into a home server

General-purpose Linux distros aren't the only way to resurrect your outdated computing companion

3
By  Ayush Pande

Any ol’ laptop can run virtual guests

If my decade-old system can do it, then so can yours

When it comes to building a home server, you’ve got a lot of options to choose from for the OS and virtualization tools. Better yet, the minimum requirements on many of these platforms are so low that you can run them on potato machines released over a decade ago. Take Proxmox, for example. As long as your laptop has an x86 processor and 1GB of memory, it’s good enough to serve as a PVE host. Source: Yours truly, who has deployed this beast of a virtualization platform on everything from an old Acer Predator Helios 300 system to a dinosaur Lenovo G510 laptop. The latter, in particular, is surprisingly decent at running containerized services – to the point where the obsolete laptop can still host a dozen apps (including those inside a nested CasaOS) before its 4GB of RAM starts to hit the red zone.

That’s a budget-friendly laptop released back in 2014. On somewhat modern laptops like my Helios 300, I can easily run a handful of virtual machines alongside LXCs without a noticeable decline in performance. For reference, neither of these laptops is qualified to run Windows 11. But arm them with Proxmox, and they turn into highly-capable workstations for server experiments.

Proxmox
See at Proxmox

You can also opt for lightweight NAS or container-only setups

Leaving Proxmox aside, you’re free to experiment with different server platforms on your old laptop. Now, I wouldn’t recommend using USB HDDs to build a Network-Attached Storage server, but if your aged laptop supports extra SATA drives, you can turn it into a decent backup rig with OpenMediaVault. In fact, repurposing systems into OMV servers is a great way to add some redundancy to your primary NAS, and you can use them as remote workstations for 3-2-1 backup pipelines if you don’t want to shell out hundreds of bucks on an offsite machine.

Alternatively, you could ditch PVE and OMV altogether and rely on a containerization platform on a low-end laptop. YunoHost and UmbrelOS are rock-solid options if you want tools that you can install the same way as a conventional ISO file, while CasaOS and Cosmos Cloud are just as effective for your container-hosting needs once you configure them on top of a pre-existing distro. Speaking of the underlying OS, you can even go for a barebones CLI distribution and equip it with only those services that you require for an efficient home server. I tend to stick with Ubuntu Server due to its terrific compatibility with most laptops, but DietPi and good ol’ Arch Linux are just as valid for your DIY workstation.

I'm addicted to installing Proxmox on old devices

Proxmox has become my favorite virtualization platform for revitalizing outdated systems

8
By  Ayush Pande

Maintenance is fairly easy on a laptop

You don’t need to connect extra peripherals to your laptop

Although laptops are undoubtedly weaker than similarly priced desktop rigs, they do have certain perks that can benefit your home lab experiments. After all, you get a built-in keyboard, mouse, and display on your laptop, meaning you don’t need to scrounge around for I/O devices or look into adapters when troubleshooting your portable workstation or tweaking its BIOS options.

Most laptops also have Wi-Fi and Bluetooth connectivity, and the latter is extremely handy when you’re planning to host Home Assistant. On paper, the built-in battery also sounds like a sweet deal, but in reality, it’s a double-edged sword that can cause more harm than good…

You’ll want to take a couple of precautions with your laptop-powered node

I recommend tossing the battery out altogether

If you’re expecting your laptop’s battery to act as a stand-in for a UPS, let me stop you there. Sure, it can keep your makeshift server active long enough for you to initiate a graceful shutdown when there’s an outage. But keeping the battery plugged in to the AC adapter at all times will cause it to swell up eventually. On a particularly old laptop, you might already have a degraded battery, and charging it 24/7 can turn it into a potential fire hazard.

Subscribe to the newsletter for practical home-lab tips

Discover practical home-lab know-how: subscribe to the newsletter for step-by-step laptop repurposing guidance, safety precautions, and lightweight OS and container options to try in your own lab.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Personally, I’ve unplugged the batteries on my old laptops, as I already have a dedicated UPS in my home lab. But if you’re not willing to get rid of the battery or have a system that doesn’t let you unmount it, you can tweak the BIOS settings to cap the max charge limit well under 80%, thereby slowing your battery’s degradation rate.

In the name of all that’s holy, please don’t close its lid

Another piece of advice I’ve often seen on forums is that you should close the laptop’s lid when it’s operational. Well, I’m here to tell you that it’s a terrible idea. Laptops tend to pull air through the keyboard, with certain models adding small vents right around the hinges. Closing the lid while your laptop is still busy with server operations will block these vents and choke the airflow. Considering how prone laptops are to thermal throttling, you really don’t want to trap the heat in their chassis. Worst-case scenario, you might end up damaging your monitor, keyboard, and laptop internals.

Subscribe to the newsletter for practical home-lab tips

But if you’re not willing to get rid of the battery or have a system that doesn’t let you unmount it, you can tweak the BIOS settings to cap the max charge limit well under 80%, thereby slowing your battery’s degradation rate.

item.title: MSI's new Claw 8 EX AI+ handheld takes on AMD-powered rivals with Intel's Arc G3 Extreme chip
item.itunes:title: MSI's new Claw 8 EX AI+ handheld takes on AMD-powered rivals with Intel's Arc G3 Extreme chip
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • MSI's new Claw 8 EX AI Plus features Intel's Arc G3 Extreme chip.
  • The handheld features an 8-inch, 1920 × 1200 pixel, 120Hz screen, Hall-Effect triggers, improved grips, and new linear-motor haptics.
  • The release date and price of the Claw 8 EX AI Plus are unknown, but it's expected to be an expensive handheld.

Ahead of Computex, MSI has revealed its new Claw 8 EX AI+ gaming handheld — yes, that really is its name (via The Verge). MSI claims the Claw 8 is the "world's first" handheld to feature Intel's new PC-handheld-focused Intel Arc G3 Extreme chip, which packs an array of Xe3 GPU cores and promises significant power-efficiency improvements.

Is MSI really the first company to reveal an Arc G3-equipped handheld? Not really. Both the Acer Predator Atlas 8 and OneXPlayer 3 were shown off earlier this week. That said, the Claw 8 might be the first PC handheld actually to release with the new CPU. Other Claw 8 EX AI Plus specs include a lone NVME M.2 SSD and up to 32GB of dual-channel LPDDR5x memory.

We don't know when MSI's Claw 8 EX AI Plus will release or how much it'll cost (but it's expected to be pricey)

The high-end handheld should offer more power and improved battery life

Other specs include an 8-inch 1920 x 1200 pixel resolution display that supports up to a 120Hz refresh rate, alongside an updated design with better grips for added comfort. Lastly, the handheld features reliable Hall-Effect triggers, a new linear motor that MSI says offers faster, improved haptic feedback, and, overall, the refreshed Claw 8 should offer better battery life. However, the company doesn't offer a battery life estimate.

It's unclear when the new Claw 8 will launch or how much it'll cost, but given MSI's previous Claw 8 AI Plus cost $1,000 when it released. However, with the recent Steam Deck price hike, I'd expect this refresh to be priced in the $1,500 range (some leaks are pointing to a pretty wild $1,900 price). The only available color option seems to be "Void Purple.

OneXPlayer's new 3-in-1 handheld has detachable controllers, liquid cooling, and a 144Hz OLED screen

And you can help fund it soon.

2
By  Simon Batt
