item.content:encoded:
Summary

  • MSI's new Claw 8 EX AI Plus features Intel's Arc G3 Extreme chip.
  • The handheld features an 8-inch, 1920 × 1200 pixel, 120Hz screen, Hall-Effect triggers, improved grips, and new linear-motor haptics.
  • The release date and price of the Claw 8 EX AI Plus are unknown, but it's expected to be an expensive handheld.

Ahead of Computex, MSI has revealed its new Claw 8 EX AI+ gaming handheld — yes, that really is its name (via The Verge). MSI claims the Claw 8 is the "world's first" handheld to feature Intel's new PC-handheld-focused Intel Arc G3 Extreme chip, which packs an array of Xe3 GPU cores and promises significant power-efficiency improvements.

Is MSI really the first company to reveal an Arc G3-equipped handheld? Not really. Both the Acer Predator Atlas 8 and OneXPlayer 3 were shown off earlier this week. That said, the Claw 8 might be the first PC handheld actually to release with the new CPU. Other Claw 8 EX AI Plus specs include a lone NVME M.2 SSD and up to 32GB of dual-channel LPDDR5x memory.

We don't know when MSI's Claw 8 EX AI Plus will release or how much it'll cost (but it's expected to be pricey)

The high-end handheld should offer more power and improved battery life

Other specs include an 8-inch 1920 x 1200 pixel resolution display that supports up to a 120Hz refresh rate, alongside an updated design with better grips for added comfort. Lastly, the handheld features reliable Hall-Effect triggers, a new linear motor that MSI says offers faster, improved haptic feedback, and, overall, the refreshed Claw 8 should offer better battery life. However, the company doesn't offer a battery life estimate.

It's unclear when the new Claw 8 will launch or how much it'll cost, but given MSI's previous Claw 8 AI Plus cost $1,000 when it released. However, with the recent Steam Deck price hike, I'd expect this refresh to be priced in the $1,500 range (some leaks are pointing to a pretty wild $1,900 price). The only available color option seems to be "Void Purple.

OneXPlayer's new 3-in-1 handheld has detachable controllers, liquid cooling, and a 144Hz OLED screen

And you can help fund it soon.

2
By  Simon Batt

item.title: I put Google's Gemma 4 on my homelab and Tailscale on my phone — and cancelled Claude Pro
item.itunes:title: I put Google's Gemma 4 on my homelab and Tailscale on my phone — and cancelled Claude Pro
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Once my daily gaming sessions dropped to just weekends, my 4070 Ti spent most of its time idle. For months, I was paying $20 for Claude Pro while a perfectly capable RTX 4070 Ti sat mostly idle in my PC. The funny part was that most of the AI prompts I sent from my phone had nothing to do with complex coding tasks or deep research. They were quick summaries, rough ideas, and random Q&A sessions throughout the day.

I had tried local models before, 7B, 12B, and even 24B, and always went back to the cloud. Their outputs were weak, the context window was tiny, and inference was slow. Then Google announced Gemma 4, and the reasons I'd left local AI behind began to fade. Not because it beats Claude Pro — it wasn’t even close — but because it replaced the part I was actually paying for. Gemma 4 e4b, a Docker container, and Tailscale on my phone — the subscription was gone.

4 Claude Code slash commands I use daily that make me more productive

Small commands, massive workflow gains.

1
By  Shekhar Vaidya

The GPU that was doing nothing finally had a job

A 4070 Ti, a 9.6GB model, and one question: why am I still paying?

When I built my PC with an RTX 4070 Ti in 2023, my motive for the build was gaming, but as time passed, and I got busier with work, my gaming sessions became fewer and fewer. While I used to game once or twice a day, now I only think about gaming on a free weekend. The 4070 Ti spent most of its life doing nothing because, in my work life as a technical writer and web developer, I rarely used the GPU to its full potential. Meanwhile, I was paying for Claude Pro every month for all types of tasks — researching, development, and day-to-day usage on my smartphone.

Previously, I had used various local AI setups and models, including 7B, 8B, 12B, and 24B, even when my PC struggled to run them properly. I left those behind because I had reasons not to use them: tiny context window, weak outputs, VRAM limitations, and slow inference. I was trying to replace cloud models with local models for the wrong reasons; there was no way a 12B local model could outrun Opus 4.7 in deep reasoning and large-project context. There were a few local models that could run neck and neck with those cloud models, but running those on a 4070 Ti was out of the question.

Last month, when Google announced Gemma 4, it was one of the few models that checked enough boxes at once. The e4b fit my 12GB VRAM GPU cleanly because of its smaller 9.6GB size. It had a 128K context window, native vision support (very useful, I'll get to why later), and its MoE architecture means only part of the model is active during inference, keeping it fast without needing all 9.6GB loaded at once. But was it a Claude Pro replacement? The answer is a big NO. When it comes to reasoning, artifacts, and coding, it was nowhere close to Claude Pro.

But were these the only use cases of Claude Pro for me? No, I also use Claude Pro on my phone and, honestly, more frequently than for coding or development. The mobile use cases for Claude Pro were things like drafting a rough idea, summarizing an online read, screenshotting something I saw on an Instagram reel and asking about it, quick Q&A at any time, and lightweight brainstorming. Most of these tasks didn’t require a frontier model like Opus 4.7 or even Sonnet 4.6. That was when I decided to replace Claude Pro on my mobile with Gemma 4. Free, private, and already sitting on my GPU. No rate limits, no usage anxiety, and the best part: everything stays local. Getting it on my phone, though, took a few more steps.

The setup took under an hour, and most of that was the download

Ollama, a Docker container, and a chat UI that looks like ChatGPT

It had been a while since I set up a local AI on my PC, and the tooling around it had improved dramatically. I expected hours of Linux tinkering, CUDA debugging, and dependency hell, but the setup ended up being surprisingly straightforward. I was already using LM Studio, but Ollama made more sense for the setup I had in mind for two reasons. First, I was planning to use Open WebUI, which is built around Ollama natively, so the setup would be simpler out of the box. Second, Ollama ran as a background service, so it started with Windows, sat in the tray, and was always ready. The setup itself was straightforward, too — a Windows installer of around 2GB, and I was done. It automatically handles the GPU detection and the CUDA setup.

The next step was to select the best Gemma 4 model for my PC, based on my mobile usage. The Gemma 4:26b was overkill and wouldn't fit in 12GB VRAM. So I decided to move ahead with gemma4:e4b — adequate 128K context window, 9.6GB in size, and both text and image support. One command in the terminal and I was done.

ollama pull gemma4:e4b

Downloading the model took longer than the setup. Once that was done, Open WebUI was another command away, since I already had Docker installed on my PC.

docker run -d -p 3000:8080 --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway -e OLLAMA_BASE_URL=http://host.docker.internal:11434 -v open-webui:/app/backend/data --name open-webui --restart always ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main

These were the only two commands needed for the entire setup. A few minutes later, the Open WebUI interface was live on https://localhost:3000. Though I did face a minor issue while running the first chat, it failed on the first attempt, and the task manager told me why. VmmemWSL was aggressively claiming memory as containers needed it, but didn't release it back to Windows when they were done. I fixed it by limiting the RAM usage via the .wslconfig file.

Once that was done, I ran the first prompt; the interface and the experience immediately felt similar to Claude. Similar model selector, familiar chat interface, conversation history, and image upload support. Watching Task Manager show 57% GPU load during inference, it was one of the times when I felt the GPU was finally doing something useful outside gaming. At that point, the setup already felt surprisingly usable from the desktop. The real shift happened when I stopped treating it like a desktop-only tool.

Tailscale is the part that actually ended the subscription

Local AI on your desk is a novelty — on your phone, it's a replacement

Okay, Ollama was set up, Gemma 4 was installed, and Open WebUI was working fine. Now what next? How would it replace Claude Pro on my mobile? That's where Tailscale came in; I was already using it on every device. So accessing Open WebUI on my phone was simple enough. I just typed the 100.x.x.x:3000 address into Safari, and it immediately loaded the UI with everything intact: the conversation history and the model; I just had to log in.

Newsletter: Practical Local AI Setup Guides

Subscribe to the newsletter for clear, reusable local-AI walkthroughs: step-by-step setup commands, model tradeoffs, cost-saving tips, and privacy-minded mobile access guidance to help you run models on your own GPU.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

But typing the Tailscale IP every time got old quickly, and then I found out Open WebUI supports PWA out of the box. From then on, it didn’t feel like a website. I added the web app to the home screen, and it started to feel like a standalone app. I could use the app even when my iPhone and PC weren't on the same network, thanks to Tailscale working flawlessly in the background. Wi-Fi was off, mobile data was on, a prompt was sent, and a response came back from my own GPU at home.

Since I was on my tailnet, I was secure anyway because there was no public exposure, and traffic was inside the Tailscale network. Even my internet behind CGNAT didn’t disturb the flow. There was only one requirement: Tailscale had to be running on the host and client devices. After a few days of use, my muscle memory started to change. I started to reach for Gemma 4 instead of Claude. That's when I realized I no longer needed Claude Pro on my phone.

I finally found a local LLM I actually want to use for coding

Qwen3-Coder-Next is a great model, and it's even better with Claude Code as a harness.

26
By  Adam Conway

Local AI didn't win. It just showed up

I didn’t cancel Claude Pro because local AI models suddenly got better. I still use Claude, but for the work that actually needs it, not for the chat subscription I mostly use on my phone. Gemma 4, Open WebUI, and Tailscale on my iPhone replaced the $20 subscription. What I gained from this setup was $240 per year, full privacy, no rate limits, and a setup that runs on hardware I already own. Local AI may not replace the frontier cloud models, but it has already become practical enough for day-to-day use.

Ollama

Ollama is a platform to download and run various open-source large language models (LLM) on your local computer. 

See at Ollama

item.title: I moved my home lab to a new house after years of 24/7 use, and here’s what broke
item.itunes:title: I moved my home lab to a new house after years of 24/7 use, and here’s what broke
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Moving is a universal headache. Nobody likes to do it, but it has to be done, and in the process of moving, a lot of things get put on the backburner. My home lab was included in that, even though it hosts a handful of important services. I didn't have time to get things fully back online until recently, and it wasn't nearly as smooth of a process as I had hoped for. After years of running, turning it off and having it be dormant for a couple of months caused a whole host of issues once I fired it back up, and during the troubleshooting process, I learned some lessons in what to do (and not to do) for next time.

3 reasons I love my Proxmox cluster (even though the average home labber doesn't need one)

A Proxmox cluster may not be all that useful for most tinkerers, but it was a fantastic addition to my home lab

5
By  Ayush Pande

No POST

The iGPU wasn't kicking in

The first failure greeted me before the network was even a question: the main box wouldn't boot. No display, no POST, just fans. I had a pretty good idea of what the cause was, though: I had pulled my GTX 1070 out of the system to move it safely, and I actually had it in my main system for some testing at the time. I didn't want to throw in either of my other modern GPUs into this thing, so I plugged a spare monitor into the iGPU and thought that'd be the end of it.

For reasons I never fully nailed down, the system wasn't falling back to the iGPU at boot, and it hung instead of coming up headless. A single red "BOOT_DEVICE" light was present on the motherboard after failing to post, which is odd, because nothing changed about the configuration besides the GPU.

Rather than chase the exact setting, I cleared CMOS to reset the board to defaults, which got it to fall back to the iGPU and POST normally. Problem solved, but it created a whole host of other issues. After trying to catch what I could, like enabling VT-d and other relevant toggles, I finally booted into Proxmox.

These 4 Proxmox mods made my PVE web UI so much better

They're pretty handy for monitoring Proxmox statistics from the web interface

1
By  Ayush Pande

Clearing CMOS broke the clock

Another small annoyance to clean up

Clearing CMOS resets more than the display behavior. It also happens to reset the real-time clock, too. So the moment the box POSTed, it came up with the wrong system date, sitting some span in the past. I didn't think anything of it until I tried to manage the node and got bounced to the Proxmox login screen. Then bounced again a few minutes later. And again. I only ever got access for a few minutes at a time before the login prompt would pop up once more.

Proxmox's web interface uses time-limited authentication tickets, and ticket validity is checked against the system clock. When the clock is wrong by a wide enough margin, the ticket fails its validity window and the UI decides your session has expired, dropping you back to the login prompt on a loop. It's an utterly maddening symptom with a rather mundane cause. Setting the date and time manually stopped it instantly, though this took some trial and error, as you can see by my command list, but that was only after I could actually get into the web UI at all.

I upgraded to Proxmox 9 - here's how it went

I was sweating bullets the entire time

2
By  Ayush Pande

New house means new router

Local address space changed

Once I got past all the headaches that come with a new ISP, the last thing I had on my mind was changing the local address space. By default, it's set at the usual 192.168.0.x range of addresses. In my old abode, the space was 10.88.111.x, and I can't explain why that is, but that's the way it was. I never bothered to change it, so everything was operating within that space, including my Proxmox host.

The node's static address lives in /etc/network/interfaces, and its hostname is mapped to that management IP in /etc/hosts. When the subnet moved, both references pointed at an address the node no longer had, and node resolution broke along with it.

The fix was to update the static address in /etc/network/interfaces to fit the new subnet and correct the hostname-to-IP line in /etc/hosts to match, then restart networking. On a single node, it's a quick edit that can be done in a few commands.

This failure was definitely super preventable. I could have reserved the same subnet on the new router or set DHCP reservations ahead of time. But you don't actually find the stale references until the node boots onto a network where they're wrong, and you definitely don't think about it when you're moving a hundred other things at the same time.

You should build a home lab if your major is even remotely associated with computing

I wouldn't have survived college without my home lab

3
By  Ayush Pande

My public services died, and there's no fixing them

My ISP uses CGNAT

Then the part I cared about most: my Minecraft and TeamSpeak servers were unreachable from the outside. No port-forwarding change brought them back, because the problem isn't on my side of the router. The new ISP puts me behind CGNAT (carrier-grade NAT) so I no longer have a dedicated public IPv4 address. The ISP shares one public address across many customers and hands me a private one in a specific range.

Subscribe to the newsletter for home-lab troubleshooting

Get focused coverage by subscribing to this newsletter: practical, hands-on guidance for home-lab moves and troubleshooting—CMOS and clock quirks, local subnet updates, CGNAT vs IPv6 workarounds, and concrete configuration fixes you can re-use.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

This is the nail in the coffin for any services I want to host that aren't standard HTTPS, really. I can't use something like a Cloudflare tunnel here, since the traffic for both Minecraft and TeamSpeak are non-HTTPS TCP/UDP traffic. I could rent a cheap VPS with a public IP, and route a WireGuard tunnel back to the lab, but I just haven't found the time.

The approach I'm most inclined to try is using IPv6, because I do have a dedicated IPv6 available to me, CGNAT-free. Minecraft works seamlessly here. You just bind the server to an IPv6 address, open the port, and publish a AAAA record in your DNS. For TeamSpeak 3, IPv6 does work and is supported, but it's finicky. For both, you end up running into the same problem: users that connect need to have IPv6 enabled themselves, which adds a bit of administrative work on my end for my friends that aren't as technically inclined as me.

These 4 Proxmox mods made my PVE web UI so much better

They're pretty handy for monitoring Proxmox statistics from the web interface

1
By  Ayush Pande

Real breakage I didn't plan for

Thankfully, my other locally hosted services were perfectly fine once I changed their respective IPs to fit in the new local address space. My real takeaway was: you can prep all you like, but you don't learn what a home lab will do until you move it. Relocation isn't a thing you prepare for so much as a thing you survive, fixing problems as they surface. And some of them, like the CGNAT wall still sitting between my servers and the internet, don't get fixed on moving day at all. They just get added to the list of home lab projects I need to finish, which, as any home labber knows, is never ending.
item.description:
Moving is a universal headache. Nobody likes to do it, but it has to be done, and in the process of moving, a lot of things get put on the backburner. My home lab was included in that, even though it hosts a handful of important services. I didn't have time to get things fully back online until recently, and it wasn't nearly as smooth of a process as I had hoped for. After years of running, turning it off and having it be dormant for a couple of months caused a whole host of issues once I fired it back up, and during the troubleshooting process, I learned some lessons in what to do (and not to do) for next time.

3 reasons I love my Proxmox cluster (even though the average home labber doesn't need one)

A Proxmox cluster may not be all that useful for most tinkerers, but it was a fantastic addition to my home lab

5
By  Ayush Pande

No POST

The iGPU wasn't kicking in

The first failure greeted me before the network was even a question: the main box wouldn't boot. No display, no POST, just fans. I had a pretty good idea of what the cause was, though: I had pulled my GTX 1070 out of the system to move it safely, and I actually had it in my main system for some testing at the time. I didn't want to throw in either of my other modern GPUs into this thing, so I plugged a spare monitor into the iGPU and thought that'd be the end of it.

For reasons I never fully nailed down, the system wasn't falling back to the iGPU at boot, and it hung instead of coming up headless. A single red "BOOT_DEVICE" light was present on the motherboard after failing to post, which is odd, because nothing changed about the configuration besides the GPU.

Rather than chase the exact setting, I cleared CMOS to reset the board to defaults, which got it to fall back to the iGPU and POST normally. Problem solved, but it created a whole host of other issues. After trying to catch what I could, like enabling VT-d and other relevant toggles, I finally booted into Proxmox.

These 4 Proxmox mods made my PVE web UI so much better

They're pretty handy for monitoring Proxmox statistics from the web interface

1
By  Ayush Pande

Clearing CMOS broke the clock

Another small annoyance to clean up

Clearing CMOS resets more than the display behavior. It also happens to reset the real-time clock, too. So the moment the box POSTed, it came up with the wrong system date, sitting some span in the past. I didn't think anything of it until I tried to manage the node and got bounced to the Proxmox login screen. Then bounced again a few minutes later. And again. I only ever got access for a few minutes at a time before the login prompt would pop up once more.

Proxmox's web interface uses time-limited authentication tickets, and ticket validity is checked against the system clock. When the clock is wrong by a wide enough margin, the ticket fails its validity window and the UI decides your session has expired, dropping you back to the login prompt on a loop. It's an utterly maddening symptom with a rather mundane cause. Setting the date and time manually stopped it instantly, though this took some trial and error, as you can see by my command list, but that was only after I could actually get into the web UI at all.

I upgraded to Proxmox 9 - here's how it went

I was sweating bullets the entire time

2
By  Ayush Pande

I never bothered to change it, so everything was operating within that space, including my Proxmox host.

I wouldn't have survived college without my home lab

3
By  Ayush Pande

Then the part I cared about most: my Minecraft and TeamSpeak servers were unreachable from the outside. No port-forwarding change brought them back, because the problem isn't on my side of the router. The ISP shares one public address across many customers and hands me a private one in a specific range.
